title: Coherent Feedback Cooling of an Ultracoherent Phononic-Crystal Membrane at Room Temperature
authors: Luiz Couto Correa Pinto Filho, Yingxuan Chen, Frederik Werner Isaksen, Daniel Allepuz-Requena, Angelo Manetta, Dennis Henneberg Høj, Ulrich Busk Hoff, Alexander Huck, Ulrik Lund Andersen
abstract: Optomechanical systems provide a versatile platform for precision measurements and investigations of fundamental physics, where bringing macroscopic resonators into the quantum regime is a widely pursued goal. Achieving such quantum behavior of solid-state mechanical resonators at room temperature would greatly broaden their applications by removing the need for cryogenic environments. Reaching this goal requires efficient cooling of mechanical motion, among various laser cooling methods, dynamical backaction cooling (DBC) is widely utilized in experiments but fundamentally limited when operating in the sideband-unresolved regime. Coherent feedback cooling (CFC) can overcome this limitation, while avoiding state collapse and the electronic restrictions inherent to measurement-based feedback. Here, we experimentally demonstrate CFC using an ultracoherent density phononic crystal membrane. By combining CFC with strong DBC in a relatively narrow cavity, we achieve a phonon occupation reduction from $5.5\times10^{6}$ to $166\pm7$, corresponding to a cooling factor of $3.3\times10^{4}$ at room temperature, even with current experimental limitations. Our results show the potential of CFC for approaching the ground state of high-$Q$ membranes at room temperature.
summary: The researchers successfully demonstrated Coherent Feedback Cooling of a mechanical membrane at room temperature, achieving a massive reduction in phonon number. This approach overcomes the limitations of standard laser cooling in the sideband-unresolved regime and provides a roadmap for reaching the quantum ground state without cryogenics.
main_problem: The challenge of cooling macroscopic mechanical resonators to the quantum regime at room temperature, specifically overcoming the fundamental limits of dynamical backaction cooling in the sideband-unresolved regime.
main_result: Experimental demonstration of Coherent Feedback Cooling (CFC) on an ultracoherent membrane at room temperature, achieving a phonon occupation reduction from 5.5e6 to 166 and an effective temperature of 9.1 mK.
method: An all-optical coherent feedback scheme where the optical probe field is re-injected after an engineered delay and displacement to provide a feedback force proportional to mechanical momentum.
model_or_system: An optomechanical system featuring a high-Q silicon nitride density phononic-crystal membrane in a Fabry-Perot cavity, involving two optical polarization modes (horizontal and vertical) due to birefringence.
key_observables: Phonon occupation number, effective temperature, mechanical position power spectral density (PSD), and phase-space uncertainty area.
important_parameters: Mechanical frequency of 1.14 MHz, quality factor of 1.1e8, cavity linewidth of 3.7 MHz, and a cooling factor of 3.3e4.
main_assumptions: The system is treated in the linearized regime; performance is currently limited by technical noise (laser-lock instability, frequency fluctuations) and in-loop optical losses.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the experimental schematic and phase-space evolution; Fig 3 shows numerical phonon occupation vs. delay/linewidth; Fig 6 shows predicted performance gains from upgrading coupling strength and reducing noise.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of room-temperature quantum optomechanics, describes the experimental setup and CFC protocol, presents the experimental results for phonon reduction, discusses the impact of technical noise, and concludes with a predictive model for future system upgrades.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems as it demonstrates a practical implementation of coherent feedback control to manipulate the steady-state of a macroscopic mechanical mode, bypassing the limits of measurement-based feedback.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Conceptual scheme of CFC. (a) Experimental schematic. The probe beam ˆℎin is sent into the optomechanical cavity. After the optomechanical interaction between the horizontally polarized intracavity field ˆℎand the membrane, the reflected output beam ˆℎout is converted to vertical polarization and combined with a strong auxiliary beam ˆ𝑣aux on a beam splitter (BS) where a displacement operation is implemented to convert optical phase information into amplitude. The resulting field is then sent through a fiber delay and re-injected into the cavity, providing a coherent feedback force on the membrane. ˆ𝑣in and ˆ𝑣denote the annihilation operators of the input cooling beam and vertically...

Fig. 2. The experiment. (a) Experimental setup for CFC scheme. A horizontally polarized probe beam is injected into the cavity containing a membrane. The reflected beam is rotated to vertical polarization by using a FR and a HWP. A small portion is directed to a balanced homodyne detector for readout, while the main beam is combined with a strong auxiliary beam on a 90:10 BS for the displacement operation. The resulting field passes through a fiber delay line before being re-injected into the cavity to provide coherent feedback. LO, local oscillator. Inset: A camera image of the Si3N4 membrane showing the phononic-crystal pattern. (b) Phononic-crystal bandgap of the membrane around the...

Fig. 3. Numerical calculations of CFC. (a) Phonon occupation ¯𝑛versus the feedback delay Ωm𝜏and the cavity linewidth 𝜅under the on-resonance condition, showing the influence of the cavity linewidth on the optimal delay. (b) Phonon occupation ¯𝑛as a function of the feedback delay Ωm𝜏and displacement angle 𝛾for 𝜅= 2𝜋× 3.7 MHz, with both optical modes on resonance. The optimal parameters are found near Ωm𝜏≈𝜋/4 and 𝛾≈−𝜋/2. (c) Phonon occupation ¯𝑛for the experimentally detunings Δℎ= −0.06𝜅 and Δ𝑣= −1.11𝜅. The optimal displacement angle shifts toward −𝜋. The unshaded region indicates the parameter space satisfying the sufficient stability criterion. Calculations above are performed using the...

Fig. 4. Experimental results of CFC. (a) Phase-quadrature power spectral densities (PSDs) of the reflected probe beam measured by homodyne detection. The blue trace (DBC1) shows the DBC spectrum with only the probe beam (cooling beam blocked) at a detuning Δℎ= −0.06𝜅and ∼50 µW input probe power. The green trace (DBC2) shows the spectrum with both probe and cooling beams (feedback signal blocked), showing enhanced DBC effect with Δ𝑣= −1.11𝜅and ∼270 µW input cooling power. The orange trace shows the spectrum with CFC with 𝛾= −153◦, Ωm𝜏= 0.24𝜋, and feedback efficiency 𝜂= 0.3. The grey trace shows the shot noise limit (SNL), normalized to 0.5. The dashed curves represent theoretical fits based...

Fig. 5. CFC as a function of detuning and delay. (a) Phonon occupation as a function of the probe detuning Δℎ/𝜅. The light grey curve shows the theoretical prediction for DBC, while the dark blue curve includes both DBC and CFC. The shaded grey area represents the uncertainty in phonon number estimated by including 10% variation in 𝜂and Ωm𝜏. Light and dark blue circles denote the experimental data for DBC and CFC, respectively. (b) Phonon occupation versus the delay Ωm𝜏with 𝛾= −0.85𝜋and Δℎ/𝜅= −0.06.
